label: Apple___Apple_scab
description: Apple scab is a fungal disease caused by Venturia inaequalis. It causes dark, scabby lesions on leaves and fruit. The fungus overwinters in fallen leaves and releases spores in spring during wet weather. Infected leaves may yellow and drop prematurely, weakening the tree.
treatment: Remove fallen leaves, apply fungicide sprays during spring, plant resistant varieties, and ensure good air circulation.
guidance: Remove fallen leaves, prune trees for airflow, and plant resistant varieties.
irrigation: Keep soil moist (2–3 cm/week); avoid wetting foliage.
fertilization: Nitrogen 80–100 kg/ha, potassium 40–60 kg/ha.

label: Apple___Black_rot
description: Black rot is caused by the fungus Botryosphaeria obtusa. It affects leaves, fruit, and bark, causing frogeye leaf spots, fruit rot, and cankers on limbs.
treatment: Prune infected branches, remove mummified fruit, and apply fungicides.
guidance: Maintain orchard sanitation and prune infected wood.
irrigation: Moderate irrigation; avoid prolonged leaf wetness.
fertilization: Balanced NPK; avoid excess nitrogen.

label: Apple___Cedar_apple_rust
description: Cedar apple rust is caused by the fungus Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae. It causes orange-yellow leaf spots and requires juniper trees to complete its life cycle.
treatment: Apply fungicides and remove nearby juniper hosts.
guidance: Plant resistant varieties and eliminate alternate hosts.
irrigation: Standard irrigation; avoid wet foliage.
fertilization: Nitrogen 70–90 kg/ha, potassium 40–50 kg/ha.

label: Apple___healthy
description: This apple leaf appears healthy with normal green coloration and no visible signs of disease or pest damage.
treatment: Maintain routine orchard care including proper pruning and nutrition.
guidance: Monitor regularly for early disease signs.
irrigation: 2–3 cm/week depending on soil type and weather.
fertilization: Balanced seasonal fertilization with NPK.

label: Blueberry___healthy
description: This blueberry leaf appears healthy with normal coloration and no visible disease symptoms.
treatment: Continue regular plant care and maintenance.
guidance: Maintain acidic soil pH (4.5-5.5) and monitor for pests.
irrigation: Keep soil consistently moist; 2.5-5 cm/week.
fertilization: Use acidifying fertilizers; avoid chloride-based products.

label: Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew
description: Powdery mildew is a fungal disease that creates white, powdery spots on leaves and shoots. It thrives in warm, dry conditions and can reduce photosynthesis and fruit quality.
treatment: Apply sulfur-based or systemic fungicides. Prune for better air circulation.
guidance: Remove infected plant parts and improve airflow between trees.
irrigation: Water at base; avoid overhead irrigation.
fertilization: Balanced NPK; avoid excessive nitrogen which promotes susceptible growth.

label: Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy
description: This cherry leaf appears healthy with normal green color and no signs of disease.
treatment: Continue standard orchard maintenance practices.
guidance: Regular monitoring and preventive care recommended.
irrigation: Deep watering weekly during dry periods.
fertilization: Apply balanced fertilizer in early spring.

label: Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot_Gray_leaf_spot
description: Gray leaf spot is caused by Cercospora zeae-maydis. It produces rectangular gray to tan lesions that run parallel to leaf veins. Severe infections can significantly reduce yield.
treatment: Plant resistant hybrids and apply fungicides if disease pressure is high.
guidance: Rotate crops and manage crop residue through tillage.
irrigation: Rain-fed or supplemental irrigation as needed.
fertilization: Balanced nitrogen management based on soil tests.

label: Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_
description: Common rust is caused by the fungus Puccinia sorghi. It produces small, circular to elongated brown pustules on both leaf surfaces. Severe infections can reduce photosynthesis and yield.
treatment: Plant resistant hybrids. Apply fungicides if rust appears before tasseling.
guidance: Scout fields regularly and consider early planting dates.
irrigation: Standard irrigation practices.
fertilization: Maintain balanced fertility for plant health.

label: Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight
description: Northern leaf blight is caused by Exserohilum turcicum. It produces long, elliptical gray-green lesions that can grow 2.5-15 cm long. Severe infections reduce yield significantly.
treatment: Use resistant hybrids and apply foliar fungicides when needed.
guidance: Rotate crops and till under crop residue.
irrigation: Maintain adequate moisture for plant health.
fertilization: Balanced NPK fertilization program.

label: Corn_(maize)___healthy
description: Healthy corn leaf with uniform green color and no visible disease symptoms.
treatment: Maintain good crop management practices.
guidance: Scout fields regularly for early disease detection.
irrigation: Adequate moisture during critical growth stages.
fertilization: Nitrogen application based on yield goals.

label: Grape___Black_rot
description: Black rot is caused by the fungus Guignardia bidwellii. It causes brown circular lesions on leaves and shriveled, black mummified fruit. The disease thrives in warm, humid conditions.
treatment: Remove mummified fruit and apply fungicides from bud break through fruit set.
guidance: Maintain good canopy management and remove infected material.
irrigation: Drip irrigation preferred to keep foliage dry.
fertilization: Balanced fertilization; avoid excess nitrogen.

label: Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles)
description: Esca is a complex fungal disease causing tiger-stripe patterns on leaves and dark spots on berries. It affects the vascular system and can cause sudden vine death.
treatment: No effective chemical control. Remove severely infected vines.
guidance: Avoid large pruning wounds and protect cuts with wound sealant.
irrigation: Maintain consistent irrigation to reduce plant stress.
fertilization: Balanced nutrition to maintain vine health.

label: Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spot)
description: Isariopsis leaf spot causes irregular brown spots with dark borders on grape leaves. Severe infections can cause premature defoliation.
treatment: Apply fungicides and remove infected leaves.
guidance: Improve air circulation and reduce humidity in canopy.
irrigation: Avoid overhead irrigation; use drip systems.
fertilization: Maintain balanced nutrition.

label: Grape___healthy
description: This grape leaf appears healthy with normal green coloration and no disease symptoms.
treatment: Continue standard vineyard management practices.
guidance: Regular scouting and preventive fungicide applications.
irrigation: Consistent watering, especially during fruit development.
fertilization: Annual soil testing and balanced fertilization.

label: Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening)
description: Huanglongbing (HLB) is a devastating bacterial disease spread by the Asian citrus psyllid. It causes blotchy mottling of leaves, lopsided bitter fruit, and eventual tree death.
treatment: No cure exists. Remove infected trees, control psyllid vectors, and use certified disease-free nursery stock.
guidance: Monitor for psyllids and implement area-wide pest management.
irrigation: Maintain tree health with proper irrigation.
fertilization: Enhanced nutrition programs may help infected trees survive longer.

label: Peach___Bacterial_spot
description: Bacterial spot is caused by Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni. It causes angular water-soaked lesions on leaves that turn brown, and raised lesions on fruit.
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides and use resistant varieties.
guidance: Avoid overhead irrigation and working with wet plants.
irrigation: Drip irrigation to keep foliage dry.
fertilization: Balanced nutrition; avoid excess nitrogen.

label: Peach___healthy
description: This peach leaf appears healthy with normal coloration and no disease symptoms.
treatment: Continue standard orchard care practices.
guidance: Regular monitoring and preventive treatments.
irrigation: Deep watering weekly during dry periods.
fertilization: Apply balanced fertilizer in early spring.

label: Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot
description: Bacterial spot is caused by Xanthomonas species. It produces small, water-soaked spots on leaves that become brown and may have yellow halos. Fruit develops raised, scabby lesions.
treatment: Use copper-based sprays, rotate crops, and plant resistant varieties.
guidance: Use disease-free seed and avoid overhead irrigation.
irrigation: Drip irrigation recommended.
fertilization: Balanced NPK; moderate nitrogen levels.

label: Pepper,_bell___healthy
description: This bell pepper leaf appears healthy with normal dark green color and no disease symptoms.
treatment: Continue standard plant care.
guidance: Monitor for pests and diseases regularly.
irrigation: Consistent moisture; 2.5-5 cm/week.
fertilization: Balanced fertilizer at planting and during fruit set.

label: Potato___Early_blight
description: Early blight is caused by Alternaria solani. It produces target-like brown spots with concentric rings on older leaves first. Severe infections can cause significant defoliation.
treatment: Apply fungicides and remove infected plant debris.
guidance: Use crop rotation and mulch soil to prevent spore splash.
irrigation: 2–3 cm/week; avoid wetting foliage.
fertilization: NPK 120:60:60 kg/ha; adequate nutrition reduces susceptibility.

label: Potato___Late_blight
description: Late blight is caused by Phytophthora infestans, the same pathogen that caused the Irish Potato Famine. It produces water-soaked lesions that rapidly expand in cool, wet conditions.
treatment: Apply fungicides preventively and remove infected plants immediately.
guidance: Rotate crops every 3–4 years and destroy volunteer plants.
irrigation: Drip irrigation preferred; avoid wet foliage.

label: Potato___healthy
description: This potato leaf appears healthy with normal green coloration and no visible disease symptoms.
treatment: Continue standard crop management practices.
guidance: Regular scouting for early disease detection.
irrigation: Consistent soil moisture during tuber development.
fertilization: Balanced fertilization based on soil tests.

label: Raspberry___healthy
description: This raspberry leaf appears healthy with normal coloration and no disease symptoms.
treatment: Continue standard cane management practices.
guidance: Prune out old canes and maintain good air circulation.
irrigation: 1-2 inches per week; drip irrigation preferred.
fertilization: Apply balanced fertilizer in early spring.

label: Soybean___healthy
description: This soybean leaf appears healthy with normal green trifoliate leaves and no disease symptoms.
treatment: Continue standard crop management.
guidance: Scout regularly for pests and diseases.
irrigation: Critical during pod fill; 1-1.5 inches/week.
fertilization: Phosphorus and potassium based on soil tests; nitrogen fixation from nodules.

label: Squash___Powdery_mildew
description: Powdery mildew appears as white, powdery fungal growth on leaf surfaces. It reduces photosynthesis and can affect fruit quality and yield.
treatment: Apply fungicides (sulfur, neem oil, or potassium bicarbonate) at first signs.
guidance: Plant resistant varieties and ensure good air circulation.
irrigation: Water at base of plants; avoid wetting foliage.
fertilization: Balanced fertilization; avoid excess nitrogen.

label: Strawberry___Leaf_scorch
description: Leaf scorch is caused by the fungus Diplocarpon earlianum. It produces irregular purple spots that enlarge and cause leaves to appear scorched or burned.
treatment: Remove infected leaves, apply fungicides, and renovate beds after harvest.
guidance: Use disease-free plants and maintain proper spacing.
irrigation: Drip irrigation to keep foliage dry.
fertilization: Balanced fertilization; avoid excess nitrogen.

label: Strawberry___healthy
description: This strawberry leaf appears healthy with normal trifoliate leaves and no disease symptoms.
treatment: Continue standard strawberry bed management.
guidance: Monitor for pests and diseases; renovate beds as needed.
irrigation: 1-1.5 inches/week; drip irrigation preferred.
fertilization: Balanced fertilizer at planting and after renovation.

label: Tomato___Bacterial_spot
description: Bacterial spot is caused by Xanthomonas species. It produces small, brown water-soaked spots with yellow halos on leaves and raised, scabby lesions on fruit.
treatment: Apply copper-based sprays and use crop rotation.
guidance: Use disease-free seed and avoid handling plants when wet.
irrigation: Drip irrigation recommended to keep foliage dry.
fertilization: Moderate nitrogen; avoid excess which promotes succulent growth.

label: Tomato___Early_blight
description: Early blight is caused by Alternaria solani. It produces dark brown spots with concentric rings (target spots) on lower leaves first, progressing upward.
treatment: Apply fungicides, remove infected leaves, and mulch around plants.
guidance: Stake plants for air circulation and rotate crops.
irrigation: Water at base; 1-2 inches/week.
fertilization: Balanced NPK; adequate calcium to prevent blossom end rot.

label: Tomato___Late_blight
description: Late blight is caused by Phytophthora infestans. It produces large, dark, water-soaked lesions that spread rapidly in cool, humid conditions and can destroy plants within days.
treatment: Remove infected plants immediately and apply fungicides preventively.
guidance: Avoid planting tomatoes near potatoes; use resistant varieties.
irrigation: 2–3 cm/week via drip irrigation.

label: Tomato___Leaf_Mold
description: Leaf mold is caused by Passalora fulva (formerly Cladosporium fulvum). It produces yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces with olive-green to brown fuzzy mold underneath.
treatment: Improve ventilation, reduce humidity, and apply fungicides.
guidance: Space plants properly and prune for air circulation.
irrigation: Drip irrigation; avoid wetting foliage.
fertilization: Balanced fertilization; avoid excess nitrogen.

label: Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot
description: Septoria leaf spot is caused by Septoria lycopersici. It produces many small, circular spots with dark borders and tan centers containing tiny black dots (pycnidia).
treatment: Remove infected leaves, apply fungicides, and mulch around plants.
guidance: Rotate crops and stake plants for better air circulation.
irrigation: Water at base of plants early in day.
fertilization: Balanced NPK fertilization.

label: Tomato___Spider_mites_Two-spotted_spider_mite
description: Two-spotted spider mites are tiny arachnids that feed on leaf undersides, causing stippling, yellowing, and bronzing. Heavy infestations produce visible webbing.
treatment: Apply miticides or insecticidal soap. Encourage beneficial predatory mites.
guidance: Keep plants well-watered as stressed plants are more susceptible.
irrigation: Maintain adequate moisture; mites thrive in hot, dry conditions.
fertilization: Avoid excess nitrogen which promotes succulent growth attractive to mites.

label: Tomato___Target_Spot
description: Target spot is caused by Corynespora cassiicola. It produces brown spots with concentric rings on leaves, stems, and fruit. It thrives in warm, humid conditions.
treatment: Apply fungicides and remove infected plant material.
guidance: Improve air circulation and avoid overhead irrigation.
irrigation: Drip irrigation preferred.
fertilization: Balanced fertilization program.

label: Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus
description: Tomato yellow leaf curl virus (TYLCV) is transmitted by whiteflies. It causes severe leaf curling, yellowing, stunting, and flower drop with little or no fruit production.
treatment: No cure. Remove infected plants, control whiteflies, and use resistant varieties.
guidance: Use reflective mulches to repel whiteflies; install insect netting.
irrigation: Standard irrigation practices.
fertilization: Maintain plant health with balanced nutrition.

label: Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus
description: Tomato mosaic virus (ToMV) causes mottled light and dark green patterns on leaves, leaf curling, and reduced fruit quality. It spreads through contaminated tools and hands.
treatment: No cure. Remove infected plants, sanitize tools, and wash hands with soap.
guidance: Use resistant varieties and virus-free seed. Avoid tobacco products near plants.
irrigation: Standard irrigation; virus not spread by water.
fertilization: Normal fertilization program.

label: Tomato___healthy
description: This tomato leaf appears healthy with normal green coloration and no visible disease or pest symptoms.
treatment: Continue proper plant care and maintenance.
guidance: Monitor weekly for early signs of problems.
irrigation: Consistent watering of 1-2 inches/week without wetting foliage.
fertilization: Balanced fertilizer program throughout the season.
